mainEntity.name: In St. George factory, they retaliated when I complained about fumes—can I sue for that too?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Retaliation for filing a comp claim or reporting safety hazards is illegal. You can pursue a wrongful termination or retaliation claim separately from your workers' comp case to recover lost wages and damages for the job loss.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Employers are strictly forbidden from retaliating against you for filing a workers' compensation claim or reporting a safety violation (like hazardous fumes). If you were fired, demoted, or had your hours cut after complaining, you may have a "wrongful termination" or "retaliatory discharge" claim. This is a separate legal action from your workers' compensation case that can result in damages for back pay and emotional distress.

Evidence of retaliation:
- The timing of the discipline (e.g., it happened right after your complaint).
- Lack of previous negative performance reviews.
- Comments from managers about "causing trouble" or "costing the company money."

Report the retaliation to the Utah Anti-Discrimination and Labor Division (UALD). Document every interaction with your manager regarding your health. A lawyer specializing in both workers' comp and employment law is crucial to handle these dual claims simultaneously.
